blocks.content: Fourth.
Cassandra stood in front of the scoreboard with the particular stillness of someone allowing a result to settle before responding to it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: We lost the memory game.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: We did.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: To two people who clearly rehearsed specifically for this bracket.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Almost certainly.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That should be — that feels like cheating.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: It's not technically—

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Don't say technically.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: It's—

She turned and looked at him with an expression of such complete, genuine betrayal that he held it for three full seconds before his face gave out. She watched the corner of his mouth start to go, and then she started laughing, and then so did he — the helpless kind that only genuinely stupid losses can produce.
Lena materialized from behind the counter with two cold cups and the satisfied expression of someone who had watched this entire sequence and found it instructive.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Fourth.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I heard.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Out of six.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I also heard that.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I'm contextualizing.
She handed them the cups.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: For the record — the memory game people come to every single bracket and I'm fairly certain this means more to them than it should. That's not a judgment. Just perspective.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That's oddly comforting.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I find it usually is.
She looked at Ethan with the appraising quiet she used when she was deciding something.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: You did well on the racing round.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Cassandra did well on the racing round.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: You didn't crash. For someone who's never played that version before, not crashing is doing well.

Cassandra was leaning against the counter with her cup, the competitive focus coming down from her face now, replaced by something looser and warmer. There was still color in her cheeks from the tournament and her hair had gotten disheveled and she looked, in that particular moment, like exactly herself.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Next bracket we practice the memory game.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I'm committing to that right now.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Good. Because we could have won. We had the racing round. We just needed the memory game.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: And the skee-ball.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: We lost skee-ball by nine points. That's recoverable.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Next time I'll tell you immediately when my aim is off.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Next time I'll be standing close enough to correct it.
Lena quietly added them to the next bracket without asking, because some things didn't require permission.
